perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own most highly confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall endeavor to protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using a degree of care it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall protect...using the same degree of care...but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care' to 'shall endeavor to protect...using a degree of care it deems appropriate' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, not a guarantee, and 'deems appropriate' introduces subjective judgment, potentially leading to inadequate protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Allowing the Recipient to decide what 'deems appropriate' is not necessarily equivalent to the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection. If the measures taken are insufficient, the Confidential Information may lose its trade secret status, harming the Owner.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy under trade secret law. The original text mandates protection 'using the same degree of care...but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care,' while the modified text only requires the Recipient to 'endeavor to protect...using a degree of care it deems appropriate.' This subjective standard may fall short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means, which includes the breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. By weakening the duty to maintain secrecy, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation and the loss of trade secret protection, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, Owner shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have, to obtain in any court of competent jurisdiction temporary, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief to restrain any breach, threatened breach, or otherwise to specifically enforce any obligations of Recipient if Recipient fails to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, Owner may request, in any court, injunctive relief if deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall have the right...to obtain' to 'may request' weakens the Owner's right to injunctive relief. Removing 'temporary, preliminary and permanent' creates ambiguity about the type of injunction available. Replacing 'competent jurisdiction' with 'any court' is too broad and might not be legally valid, as not all courts have jurisdiction over the matter. Finally, the qualifier 'if deemed necessary' adds a subjective element, diminishing the Owner's recourse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington Revised Code (RCW) 7.40.020 - Grounds for injunction
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 7.40.020 outlines specific grounds for obtaining an injunction in Washington. The modified text's 'if deemed necessary' clause is vague and does not align with the specific and established legal grounds for injunctive relief. A court may not grant an injunction based solely on the Owner's subjective determination of necessity, particularly if it doesn't meet the criteria outlined in RCW 7.40.020.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 7.40.020: Grounds for issuance. Print RCWs > Title 7 > Chapter 7.40 > Section 7.40.020 7.40.010 << 7.40.020 >> 7.40.030 PDF RCW 7.40.020 Grounds for issuance. When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of some act, the commission or continuance of which during the litigation would produce great injury to the plaintiff; or when during the litigation, it appears that the defendant is doing, or threatened, or is about to do, or is procuring, or is suffering some act to be done in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action tending to render the judgment ineffectual; or where such relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining proceedings upon any final order or judgment, an injunction may be granted to restrain such act or proceedings until the further order of the court, which may afterwards be dissolved or modified upon motion. And where it appears in the complaint at the commencement of the action, or during the pendency thereof, by affidavit, that the defendant threatens, or is about to remove or dispose of his or her property with intent to defraud his or her creditors, a temporary injunction may be granted to restrain the removal or disposition of his or her property. [ 2011 c 336 s 194 ; Code 1881 s 154; 1877 p 33 s 154 ; 1869 p 38 s 152 ; 1854 p 152 s 112 ; RRS s 719.]
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided the owner with a defined right to obtain injunctive relief in specific courts and circumstances. The modified contract weakens this right by changing 'shall have the right...to obtain' to 'may request,' introduces a subjective element with 'if deemed necessary,' and removes specific criteria (temporary, preliminary, permanent) for the injunctive relief. The Law Explanation highlights that RCW 7.40.020 outlines specific grounds for obtaining an injunction, and the 'if deemed necessary' clause doesn't align with these established legal grounds. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RCW 7.40.020, states that an injunction may be granted when the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded and the commission of an act would produce great injury to the plaintiff. The modified text undermines the owner's ability to obtain injunctive relief based on the specific grounds outlined in RCW 7.40.020 because the 'if deemed necessary' clause introduces an element of subjectivity that isn't compatible with the statute's requirement for specific findings of potential injury.
